title: Switch: How to Change Things When Change Is Hard
author: Chip Heath, Dan Heath
excerpt: Why is change so hard? The Heath brothers answer with a powerful framework: the rational Rider, the emotional Elephant, and the situational Path. To change behavior, you must direct the Rider, motivate the Elephant, and shape the Path.
keyIdeas: The Rider (rational mind) and the Elephant (emotional mind) — direct the Rider, motivate the Elephant, shape the Path
Bright spots are the most efficient starting point for change; find what works and amplify it
Script the critical moves — ambiguity is the enemy of change
Shrink the change to build momentum; small wins create the confidence to tackle bigger challenges
keyTakeaways: Change is hard because the rational and emotional minds are often in conflict — successful change requires engaging both
Instead of analyzing problems exhaustively, look for bright spots — what is already working and how can you do more of it?
Make the path easier by tweaking the environment, not by demanding more willpower from people
Growth mindset: expect failure en route, not of the mission
whoShouldRead: Managers and leaders driving organizational change
Anyone trying to make personal changes that require sustained effort
Readers of Made to Stick who want the Heath brothers on change
whoShouldSkip: Those looking for an individual habit-formation system
Readers who prefer data over stories
